does: This paper introduces Selective Preference Optimization (SePO), a method that improves large language model alignment by selectively optimizing only key tokens rather than all tokens. The core innovation is a token selection strategy based on Direct Preference Optimization that trains a small oracle model to estimate token-level reward functions from response-level preference annotations. The oracle estimates which tokens are most important for alignment by scoring all tokens in the target dataset, and only high-scoring key tokens supervise the main policy model using a reference model-free contrastive objective. This approach achieves fine-grained preference optimization while reducing computational cost and noise compared to methods that optimize all tokens uniformly.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in token-level alignment methods that provide more granular supervision than response-level approaches, extending the Direct Preference Optimization framework that has become central to modern LLM alignment. It responds to limitations in existing selective alignment strategies, which either waste computation on noisy tokens or rely on expensive key token selection mechanisms. The paper combines insights from preference-based fine-tuning with efficient selection strategies, drawing on the broader literature of importance-weighted learning and active learning in machine learning. By leveraging DPO to estimate reward functions at the token level, it adapts insights from reinforcement learning and inverse reward learning to the alignment setting.
enables: This work opens several directions for future research in efficient LLM alignment. The token-level reward estimation framework could be extended to other alignment objectives beyond preference optimization, and the oracle-based selection strategy might inspire more sophisticated methods for identifying training-critical tokens. The approach enables practical large-scale alignment with reduced computational requirements, making fine-grained preference optimization accessible with smaller models and datasets. Future work could explore learned selection criteria that adapt to different domains, combine multiple selection signals, or apply the framework to multimodal models. The method also provides a foundation for understanding which tokens actually matter for behavioral alignment in language models.
